one_line_takeaway: This SoK's own contribution is a phase-aligned (preprocessing / federated fine-tuning / inference) taxonomy mapping healthcare-LLM adversaries, attack surfaces, and privacy-preserving defenses to their specific residual limitations, concluding that all current PPTs (anonymization, synthetic data, DP, SMPC, secure aggregation, LoRA-based compression, KV-cache protection, MPC/HE) are only partial and phase-agnostic, motivating combined, phase-tied defense recommendations rather than any single technique.
key_finding: Across all three phases the paper's central, repeated finding is that existing privacy-preserving techniques provide only partial, phase-agnostic protection and leave specific vulnerabilities from the paper's own threat model unaddressed: (1) Preprocessing -- anonymization, synthetic data generation, and differential privacy each fail to stop data poisoning injected before the defense is applied, and none fully close log-based PHI leakage or high-resource-adversary re-identification via quasi-identifiers. (2) Federated fine-tuning -- DP degrades rare-disease/small-cohort fidelity while residual signal in cached tensors still enables gradient inversion and membership inference; secure aggregation hides updates only at the aggregator and does not stop poisoning/backdoors or endpoint-level leakage (optimizer-state dumps); weight-delta sharing is reconstructable across multiple rounds without added DP/clipping; blockchain-based provenance gives tamper-evidence but not confidentiality, since ledger/audit metadata itself can be cross-linked to re-identify rare-disease cohorts. (3) Inference -- local DP and input obfuscation reduce leakage but degrade utility or are bypassed by cross-session correlation; KV-cache protections (KV-Shield, PFID/TEEs) depend on trusted hardware and persistent/misconfigured caches can still expose full session transcripts; cryptographic protocols (MPC/HE) introduce timing side channels exploitable over WAN links; operational logging/provenance traces persistently retain PHI absent strict minimization. The paper's contribution is this systematic per-technique gap analysis and the resulting phase-tied recommendations (e.g., combining adaptive DP with randomized LoRA for gradient leakage, pairing secure aggregation with clipping and anomaly detection for update leakage, combining MPC with GPU-accelerated HE and TEE-based local early-layer execution for hybrid inference).
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) that organizes the privacy/security literature on healthcare LLMs into a phase-aligned taxonomy across three operational phases -- Data Preprocessing, Federated Fine-Tuning, and Inference. For each phase the paper's own contribution is a structured mapping of attacker landscape (internal vs. external adversaries with a low/moderate/high prior-knowledge-and-capability gradient) -> attack-surface vulnerabilities -> enabled attacks -> existing privacy-preserving techniques (PPTs) -> per-technique limitations -> phase-tied recommendations, consolidated in Tables 3 (Preprocessing), 4 (Federated Fine-Tuning), and 5 (Inference). The authors claim novelty relative to six prior surveys ([7]-[12], compared in Table 2) on the grounds that those surveys cover phases only partially and treat threat models narrowly or partially, whereas this SoK claims full three-phase coverage, a phase-mapped threat model, and phase-tied recommendations tied back to specific limitations.
threat_model: Adversaries are categorized per phase along two axes: location (internal, i.e., clinicians/annotators, data engineers/ML staff, system administrators/IT; vs. external, i.e., ransomware groups, corporate/state-linked actors, network intruders, API-based attackers) and a capability gradient (low-resource insiders who leak data unintentionally through routine workflows; moderate-resource insiders/engineers with access to staging data, gradients, or checkpoints; high-resource externals who combine auxiliary datasets or infrastructure access for targeted exploitation). Preprocessing-phase vulnerabilities: quasi-identifiers in raw EHR/HL7/FHIR data, PHI leakage via ETL/debug logs, weak access controls/APIs, and data poisoning of clinical feeds. Federated fine-tuning vulnerabilities: model gradient leakage, client update leakage, communication-channel interception, and misconfigured audit/logging systems, enabling gradient inversion, membership inference, update-leakage attacks, model poisoning/backdoors, and communication-layer interception. Inference-phase vulnerabilities: prompt/hidden-state leakage, KV-cache leakage, leakage via returned text, delegated edge-cloud inference exposure, restoration/meta-vector channels, and operational logging/provenance traces, enabling prompt reconstruction, embedding inversion, output-based inference (e.g., cohort membership inference), split-inference intercept attacks, restoration-vector attacks, and log-correlation attacks.
limitations: As a review/SoK paper, no results are the authors' own empirical measurements -- all defenses, attacks, and named systems (e.g., DP-Forward, InferDPT, PrivacyRestore, KV-Shield, PFID, PermLLM, eFedLLM, PrivateLoRA) are drawn from cited third-party work, and the paper does not run or reproduce any of them. The literature search, while structured, is not a formally registered systematic review (no PRISMA-style protocol, no inter-rater reliability reporting for inclusion decisions) and the authors acknowledge their scope is intentionally narrowed to text-generating/text-consuming LLM pipelines, excluding imaging-only models unless they touch PHI-bearing text. The novelty claim over six prior surveys (Table 2) rests on the authors' own qualitative scoring (Full/Partial/Narrow/Absent) of those surveys, which is inherently subjective and not independently validated.
